text: Mark PalTurn knowledge into top marks. An AI exam prep platform that teaches exam technique, not just content. -- 1 of 17 -- The Problem & Value Proposition Students don’t fail exams because they don’t know the content, they fail because they don’t know how to answer questions. Teachers spend months delivering content. Students revise for hundreds of hours. Yet every year, thousands still fall short - not because they’re unprepared, but because they write answers that don’t match the mark scheme. I know this firsthand. In my GCSE Chemistry, I went from a grade 4 to a grade 8 in just 8 months without learning new content. The only thing I changed was how I answered questions, using past papers to understand how examiners think. This insight changed my grade - Mark Pal scales that to millions. The Problem -- 2 of 17 -- The Solution MarkPal is an AI-powered exam preparation tool that shows students exactly how to answer questions the way examiners award marks. Upload or type an exam question Write your answer MarkPal scores it against the official mark scheme AI highlights why marks were lost and how to improve Students learn technique, not just content We turn knowledge into higher grades, bridging the gap that traditional revision tools leave wide open. Mark Pal doesn’t replace content platforms. It makes them effective. -- 3 of 17 -- The Product (MVP) Our MVP is completed. Students uploads a question, answer, and mark scheme, and MarkPal uses AI to grade and explain feedback step by step. It shows: ✅ Marks awarded per point 🔎 Where marks were lost 🧠 How to improve next time It’s simple, fast, and designed to feel like a real exam, with AI acting as the examiner. Student uploads → AI grades → feedback → improved attempt. Next 3 Features: multi-subject support, universal question bank adaptive question sets. -- 4 of 17 -- Mark Pal In Action (Product Demo) https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zto7WQ8enBw -- 5 of 17 -- Why Now? MarkPal’s AI now marks student answers like a real examiner, awarding points, highlighting lost marks, and showing how to improve. Exam pressure is at record highs, and grade boundaries are tightening yearly. Yet platforms still focus on what to learn, not how to answer. Grand View Research -- 6 of 17 -- Market Size Total Addressable Market: 2024: $163.5B 2030 (projected): $348.8B CAGR (2025-30): 13.3% Source: Grand View Research Serviceable Available Market (AI in Education): 2024: $5.88B 2030 (projected): $32.27B CAGR (2025-30): 31.2% Source: Grand View Research Serviceable Obtainable Market (UK Secondary & College Education): Target Audience: 3M+ students (GCSE/A-Level) Initial Focus: Students and parents seeking affordable, effective exam preperation tools Capturing just 10% of UK GCSE/A-Level students (~300,000 users) at £18 ARPU = £65M ARR. Even a 0.5% wedge (~15,000 users) creates £3.2M ARR - enough to become a valuable acquisition target for Seneca, Quizlet, or tutoring platforms This UK wedge is Phase 1. The long-term play is global: English-speaking markets (US, CA, AUS, etc.) follow the same structured exam format with technique-driven outcomes. TAM expands 10x with product localisation. -- 7 of 17 -- The Business Model Freemium to paid subscriptions: Basic, Pro, Pro+ Pricing Strategy: Pro+ slightly above Pro (£5 difference) to encourage upsell Added 1-account-creation-cap-per-device system to prevent abuse Future B2B: school licenses, high-margin recurring revenue We expect 4–5% of free users to convert to paid (benchmarking against Duolingo and Quizlet freemium rates). -- 8 of 17 -- Tier Monthly Price Share of Paid Users Notes Basic £10 30% Entry-level, core access Pro £20 50% Most common upgrade Pro+ £25 20% Live AI Analysis & Improvement System Pricing Tiers & ARPU Strategy Blended ARPU: ~£18/month Churn is aligned with the academic calendar. Students typically stay active for 5–6 months leading up to exams. However, new cohorts reset the user base every year, creating a predictable, repeatable acquisition cycle and some students choose to start exam practice early (up to 3 years). Upsell strategy focuses on driving Pro+ adoption with exclusive, high-utility features like personalised question uploads and AI feedback. Based on usage patterns and ARPU, we estimate an average LTV of ~£90–£108 per user. This supports a target CAC of < £10, delivering a healthy LTV:CAC ratio of 9:1 at scale. -- 9 of 17 -- Phase Timeline Users Paid Users MRR Goal Notes MVP Beta Month 0–2 5,000 250 £5.2K Facebook Ads Community Growth Month 3–6 25,000 1250 £26K TikTok, FB Mums, GCSE creators Paid Acquisition Month 6–9 60,000 3000 £63K Targeted Instagram & YouTube ads B2B Pilots Month 9–12 120,000 6000 £126K Begin school deals, group access Expansion Month 12–18 200,000 10,000 £210K Strong referral loops kick in Strategic Growth Month 18–24 300,000 15,000 £315K Strong MRR + product defensibility Go-To-Market Strategy Target CAC: < £10 Target LTV: ~£100 (5–6 month average lifetime) In early stages, marketing is led by the founder (Tomas) through Facebook Ads. A growth intern will be brought on within the first 3 months (see Hiring Plan). -- 10 of 17 -- Period Active Users Paid Users MRR (£) ARR (£) Burn (£/mo) Month 0–2 5,000 200–250 £4.2K-£5.25K £50K–£63K £2.5K Month 3–6 25,000 1,000-1,250 £21K-£26K £252K-£315K £3.5K Month 6–9 60,000 2,400-3,000 £50K-63K £605K-£756K £5K Month 9–12 120,000 4,800-6,000 £100K-£126K £1.2M-£1.5M £6K Month 12–18 200,000 8,000-10,000 £168K-£210K £2.0M-£2.5M £7K Month 18–24 300,000 12,000-15,000 £252K-£315K £3.0M-£3.8M £8K Financial Projections With a low price point (£21/month), high-stakes problem (exam performance), and real AI differentiation, we expect 4–5% conversion rates - 2–3x industry averages. Primary Milestone: Prove 4-5% free to paid conversion at small scale (5K-10K users). Secondary Milestone: Reach £20K-30K MRR with repeatable paid acquisition. -- 11 of 17 -- Quarter Feature Release Q3 2026 Subject & Question Bank Expansion – Add heavily essay-based subjects: English, History, Business. Launch feedback tuning engine for long-form answers, and a universal question bank with real exam questions, already preloaded from official exam boards Develop a Mobile-Native App - Deliver fully featured mobile experience to expand usage among younger students and widen accessibility. Q4 2026 B2B Pilot Launch - Roll out MarkPal to schools and private tutors via group accounts and management dashboards. Begin onboarding early education partners. Q4 2026 Feature Deepening + Global Prep - Improve AI grading accuracy via feedback data. Add performance tracking dashboard. Prep for localisation. Q1 2027 International Localisation - Launch tailored versions for SAT, IB, and Australian exam boards. Adapt feedback to regional mark schemes. Roadmap -- 12 of 17 -- MarkPal is the only platform focused on exam technique, not content. Total Addressable Market: Seneca, Quizlet, traditional tutoring platforms Our Wedge: Entry point is high-stakes UK GCSE & A-Level exams, where mark schemes, not knowledge, determine outcomes. Our Moat: AI trained directly on mark schemes. Feedback engine improves with every student answer Dataset becomes proprietary and difficult to replicate Feature set focused on technique - not just recall Feature Seneca Quizlet MarkPal Content revision ✅ ✅ ✅ AI feedback on answers ❌ ❌ ✅ Mark scheme-based grading ❌ ❌ ✅ Personalised technique tips ❌ ❌ ✅ Technique-focused progress ❌ ❌ ✅ Competitors & Our Defensible Wedge Expansion Strategy Once the wedge is proven in UK secondary exams, we expand by: Adding more subjects (Science, Maths, etc.) Entering other structured-exam markets (US SAT, IB, Australia, India) Licensing AI feedback engine to schools (B2B) -- 13 of 17 -- Role Purpose Timeline Growth Intern / Lead Run TikTok, Discord, Reddit growth 0–3 months Curriculum Advisor Align with GCSE/A-Level mark schemes Advisory (in progress) Freelance UI/UX Designer Improve onboarding + mobile flow 3–6 months Part-time Support User feedback + scale ops 6–9 months Founder & Hiring Plan Tomas Jones (Founder & CEO) Exited Scrapr.io, a B2B SaaS built solo and sold at 17 Built 5+ AI-powered apps end-to-end across SaaS, EdTech, and finance Most recently: a quantitative FX research LLM built in Python Full-stack + ML developer (Python, Next.js, LangChain, OpenAI, Postgres) GCSE student turned founder, solving the exam technique gap from experience Hiring Plan (Next 6-12 Months) -- 14 of 17 -- Financials We’re raising £250K pre-seed on a SEIS/EIS-eligible SAFE at a £2.5M post-money valuation cap. Implied investor ownership at cap: 10% Target: £200K+ MRR within 18 months. £300K+ MRR by month 24. Open to strategic equity or convertible note if aligned. Use of funds: £100K - Product development (AI, new subjects) £100K - Go-to-market and user growth £50K - Content, operations, and hires This raise funds validtion of the conversion model, paid acquisition channels, and product retention before aggressive scale. -- 15 of 17 -- Exit Strategy MarkPal is built for strategic acquisition by incumbent EdTech platforms once the UK GCSE and A-Level wedge is proven. By Month 18 to 24, MarkPal is projected to reach 12,000 to 15,000 paid users, generating £252K to £315K MRR, equivalent to £3.0M to £3.8M ARR. At this scale, MarkPal becomes a natural acquisition target for revision platforms, tutoring marketplaces, and school software providers seeking AI-driven assessment and exam-marking capability. The company’s value is driven by a defensible focus on exam technique, AI trained on official mark schemes, and a growing proprietary feedback dataset. Comparable EdTech SaaS acquisitions at this stage typically transact at 5x to 8x ARR, implying a target exit valuation of £15M to £30M. At a £15M exit, a 10% stake returns £1.5M (6x), and at a £30M exit, a 10% stake returns £3M (12x). The expected exit window is 24 to 36 months following the pre-seed raise -- 16 of 17 -- Next Steps We’re raising £250K pre-seed to launch MarkPal to hundreds of thousands of students and prove what’s possible when AI teaches exam technique, not just content. We’ve built the MVP, and now we’re ready to go to market. Let’s chat if you want to help 3M+ UK students turn knowledge into top marks. Email: tomasjonesdev@gmail.com LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/tomas-jones1/ -- 17 of 17 --

analysis.summary: MarkPal addresses a clear problem in exam preparation by focusing on teaching exam technique using AI, which is a distinct and promising value proposition. The deck demonstrates understanding of the market opportunity with detailed segmentation and revenue projections. The MVP is built and demonstrates a core product offering with a clear go-to-market strategy and financial forecast. However, certain critical areas such as detailed traction data, comprehensive team capabilities beyond the founder, and deeper competitive analysis lack depth. Financial assumptions, while optimistic, need validation through early user traction. The fundraising ask and use of funds are clearly stated. Overall, the deck positions MarkPal moderately well but requires more proof points on user engagement, scalability, competitive defensibility, and team strength to be investor-ready.

categoryScores.feedback: The problem is clearly articulated and compelling, backed by founder's personal insight and validated by broad student challenges in exam technique rather than content knowledge.
Solution addresses the problem with a novel AI-powered approach targeting exam technique, not just content. The value proposition is well defined and differentiated from competitors.
Strong market sizing with credible sources and segmentation. Focus on UK GCSE/A-Level is a realistic entry point with international expansion plans. TAM, SAM, SOM are well articulated.
MVP completed and core functionality demonstrated. Upcoming features and roadmap show thoughtful product evolution. However, demo linked but no explicit usage data or user feedback provided.
Initial beta user numbers and conversion assumptions are stated, but no actual traction metrics, engagement, retention, or revenue data provided. Early traction proof is weak or missing.
Freemium to subscription model is standard and supported by credible ARPU and LTV:CAC assumptions referencing comparable platforms. Future B2B is a good extension.
Marketing channels and early strategies are outlined, including founder-led acquisition and internship plans, but lack specific performance metrics and detailed user acquisition cost validation.
Competitive landscape presented with differentiation claims. However, deeper comparative data, potential competitor risks, and barriers to entry insights are needed to strengthen defensibility.
Founder background is strong with relevant technical and domain experience. Additional team members and advisory roles are minimal or in progress, providing limited broader team validation.

Detailed financial projections, pricing, and burn rates are presented. Assumptions are optimistic but reasonable. However, no financial history or current revenue figures are shown to validate forecasts.
Clear pre-seed raise amount, valuation, equity offered, and planned use of funds. Exit strategy and investor return scenarios communicated transparently.

strengths: Clear articulation of a unique problem and solution focused on exam technique rather than content.
Demonstrated MVP with AI-enabled marking and feedback capabilities.
Detailed market sizing and realistic phased go-to-market plan focused on UK exams.
Credible business model with thoughtful pricing tiers and revenue projections.
Transparent fundraising plan with clear use of funds and exit strategy.
weaknesses: No current traction metrics such as active users, retention, or revenue to validate assumptions.
Limited team beyond the founder and few advisors, raising execution risk concerns.
Competition analysis lacks depth about barriers and potential competitive response.
Go-to-market execution risks are unmitigated with limited early user acquisition data.
Financial forecasts are optimistic without early validating evidence.
analysis.investorReadiness: Moderately ready for early stage investment but requires validation of user traction, expansion of team, and deeper competitive insight to reduce execution risk and support financial projections.

suggestedFixes.title: Showcase Early User Metrics and Testimonials
suggestedFixes.explanation: Demonstrate active user numbers, retention rates, paid conversion, and user feedback from MVP or beta to validate market interest and financial assumptions.

suggestedFixes.title: Expand Team and Advisory Details
suggestedFixes.explanation: Provide detailed profiles of current and planned hires, advisors, and their relevant expertise to strengthen execution credibility.

suggestedFixes.title: Deepen Competitive Analysis
suggestedFixes.explanation: Include a more thorough competitor landscape with barriers to entry, potential competitive threats, and defensibility strategy.

suggestedFixes.title: Present Go-to-Market Performance Data
suggestedFixes.explanation: Add data or case studies showing marketing channel effectiveness and realistic CAC to support the customer acquisition strategy.

suggestedFixes.title: Outline Risks and Mitigation Strategies
suggestedFixes.explanation: Address potential product, market, and operational risks with plans to reduce them to reassure investors about managing setbacks.

priorityActions.action: Validate user acquisition and conversion via initial beta or pilot programs.
priorityActions.reason: Building evidence of market demand and financial projection accuracy reduces investor risk and supports growth plans.

priorityActions.action: Strengthen and expand the team beyond the founder to cover marketing, product, and operations roles.
priorityActions.reason: A well-rounded team is critical for execution and scaling post-fundraising.

priorityActions.action: Develop and deepen competitor analysis and defensibility narrative.
priorityActions.reason: To prepare for investor due diligence and competitive dynamics during scaling phases.

fundraisingRisks: Lack of demonstrated early user traction increases execution risk and could dissuade investor confidence.
Small founding team and limited advisory support increases operational risk.
Highly competitive EdTech landscape with entrenched players could limit market capture.
Optimistic financial assumptions rely heavily on acquiring and converting users quickly, which is unproven.
Potential product and technical risks related to AI grading accuracy and adaption to multiple exam boards.

text: MAY 2026 INVESTOR PRESENTATION A disciplined plan to acquire and operate small recurring-revenue software products. Prepared by Tomas Jones Basingstoke, United Kingdom CONFIDENTIAL -- 1 of 15 -- 02 / 15 STANDEN CAPITAL · CONFIDENTIAL May 2026 Standen Capital · Basingstoke, UK 01 · CONTEXT The Opportunity Small software products are mispriced. The fix is execution, not invention. THE INEFFICIENCY — Too small for institutional private equity. Too operationally detailed for passive buyers. — Founders are tired. Products are neglected. Revenue is real and recurring. — Weak onboarding, stale pricing, no analytics, bloated tooling, high support load. — The work is practical. Not a rebuild. Not speculation. Just better execution. MARKET REFERENCE POINTS $23.8B UK SAAS MARKET 2024 Grand View Research $40.4B FORECAST UK SAAS BY 2030 8.5% CAGR · 2025-30 3.9x MEDIAN SAAS SALE MULTIPLE Acquire.com · 2024-25 The model is built around operating leverage, not speculative venture outcomes. -- 2 of 15 -- 03 / 15 STANDEN CAPITAL · CONFIDENTIAL May 2026 Standen Capital · Basingstoke, UK 02 · THESIS Five Operating Levers We buy under-managed profit streams and make them cleaner. 01 Cost Control Remove redundant tools. Optimise hosting. Automate first-line support. 02 Retention Add onboarding flows. Track usage. Renewal prompts. At-risk flags. 03 Pricing Replace flat plans with clear tiers. Annual billing. Higher-value packages. 04 Product Quality Fix high-friction workflows. Improve activation points. No rebuilds. 05 Exit Readiness Monthly reporting. Documented systems. Clean data room. Buyer narrative. ECONOMIC RESULT Higher operating margin Longer customer lifetime Higher ARPU & cash flow Higher conversion & retention Higher buyer confidence -- 3 of 15 -- 04 / 15 STANDEN CAPITAL · CONFIDENTIAL May 2026 Standen Capital · Basingstoke, UK 03 · FILTER Acquisition Criteria What the first deal looks like — and what it cannot be. REQUIRED Total spend cap £50,000 all-in MRR range £2,000 — £5,000 Customer count 50+ paying customers Customer concentration No single customer > 15% Revenue history 12+ months verified Monthly logo churn Below 7% Technology Modern, maintainable stack Reason for sale Founder fatigue or new project REJECT × Unclear IP ownership × Heavy paid-acquisition dependence × Products needing a full rebuild × Major customer concentration × Weak payment evidence × Large support debt × Platform dependency risk × Unclear compliance status The first deal must be boring enough to underwrite and small enough to improve without external rescue capital. -- 4 of 15 -- 05 / 15 STANDEN CAPITAL · CONFIDENTIAL May 2026 Standen Capital · Basingstoke, UK 04 · SOURCING Finding the Deal Before the Auction Marketplaces give coverage. The edge is direct outreach to neglected products. EXPECTED FIRST-ACQUISITION FUNNEL SCREENED 120 DILIGENCED 24 OFFERS MADE 10 LOIS SIGNED 4 CLOSED 1 SOURCING CHANNELS Marketplaces Acquire.com, Flippa, brokers. Fast access to listed assets and price benchmarks. Direct outreach Founders with neglected products, stale updates, multiple projects. Lower competition. Directory research Niche tools with paying users but weak positioning. Found before they are listed. Standen network Existing software conversations. Higher trust and technical credibility. -- 5 of 15 -- 06 / 15 STANDEN CAPITAL · CONFIDENTIAL May 2026 Standen Capital · Basingstoke, UK 05 · CAPITAL The First-Deal Envelope A strict £50,000 all-in cap. No hidden second budget. Acquisition consideration £42,000 84% Purchase of product, codebase, domain, customer accounts, brand, operating assets. Legal & administration £3,000 6% Asset purchase agreement, IP assignment, data processing review, accounting setup. Technical migration £2,000 4% Hosting, analytics, monitoring, support documentation, tooling switchovers. Operating reserve £3,000 6% Short reserve for first 60-90 days while systems are stabilised. Total maximum spend £50,000 If a target cannot be bought and stabilised within this limit, it is not the right first target. -- 6 of 15 -- 07 / 15 STANDEN CAPITAL · CONFIDENTIAL May 2026 Standen Capital · Basingstoke, UK 06 · WORKED EXAMPLE BriefStack A fictional target showing exactly the profile Standen Capital wants to acquire. A simple client brief and approval platform for small marketing teams. The founder built it as a side project, grew it through content and referrals, then stopped actively improving it after taking a full-time role. Not broken. Has customers. Has recurring revenue. The founder has left value on the table. BRIEFSTACK AT ACQUISITION £3,250 MRR £39,000 ARR 108 CUSTOMERS £30.09 ARPU / MONTH 4.8% MONTHLY LOGO CHURN £1,820 MONTHLY COSTS £1,430 MONTHLY PROFIT 2.45x ENTRY MULTIPLE Proposed acquisition price: £42,000 Total first-deal spend within £50,000 cap -- 7 of 15 -- 08 / 15 STANDEN CAPITAL · CONFIDENTIAL May 2026 Standen Capital · Basingstoke, UK 07 · EXECUTION The First 100 Days Control before growth. Four phases. Clear outputs. 01 DAYS 1—14 Control & Handover Receive all access: code, hosting, payments, analytics. Record walkthroughs. Build asset register and baseline operating report. 02 DAYS 15—45 Cost & Infrastructure Reset Remove avoidable spend. Simplify support tooling. Install monitoring. Reduce monthly costs from £1,820 to ~£1,150. 03 DAYS 46—75 Retention & Onboarding Structured onboarding. Activation checklist. Health scores. At-risk alerts. Cancellation surveys. Fix activation gaps. 04 DAYS 76—100 Pricing & Packaging Replace flat pricing with Starter, Growth, Studio tiers. Introduce annual billing. analysis.summary: Standen Capital presents a disciplined and well-structured business model focused on acquiring and efficiently operating small SaaS products with recurring revenue. The deck clearly outlines the problem of mispriced and neglected small software assets, a repeatable operating thesis, strict acquisition criteria, phased execution plan, financial projections, risk mitigations, and staged scaling plan. However, the deck lacks concrete traction data (no real acquisitions yet), limited information on the founding team and their experience, and absence of detailed fundraising ask including terms. Financials are modeled but based on a fictional example with no historical performance. The go-to-market strategy leans heavily on acquisition sourcing which is adequate but could be more quantitatively proven. Overall, the approach is conservative and investor-centric with clear downside caps, but real validation is pending first acquisition.

Solution is execution focused with practical operating levers; no speculation or rebuilds needed.

No proprietary product is detailed; model relies on acquiring existing products. Product quality levers described.
No actual acquisitions completed; only a fictional example shown. Early stage with no proof of concept.
Clear model of small acquisitions with operational improvements driving returns; well-articulated.
Detailed sourcing channels described with funnel metrics, but lacks historical conversion data or pipeline validation.
Competitive landscape not addressed explicitly; implicit disadvantages of institutional buyers noted.
No information on team background, experience, or capability to execute the model.
Financial model presented with clear milestones, but based on a hypothetical asset and conservative assumptions.
Funding needs are outlined (£50k for first acquisition), but lacks detail on investor terms, equity offered, or use beyond first deal.
